sentences.base_language: My name is Sofía. I live in a small apartment near the city centre.
sentences.target_language: 私の名前はソフィアです。市の中心部の近くにある小さなアパートに住んでいます。
sentences.romanized: Watashi no namae wa Sofia desu. Shi no chūshinbu no chikaku ni aru chīsana apāto ni sunde imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 住んでいます (sunde imasu) = I live (ongoing state). The 〜ている form expresses both actions in progress AND ongoing states. 住む (sumu) always uses this form for residence: 東京に住んでいます (I live in Tokyo).

sentences.base_language: Every morning I wake up, eat bread with butter and drink coffee.
sentences.target_language: 毎朝、目が覚めて、バターを塗ったパンを食べてコーヒーを飲みます。
sentences.romanized: Maiasa, me ga samete, batā wo nutta pan wo tabete kōhī wo nomimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 目が覚める (me ga sameru) = to wake up (literally 'eyes open/clear'). This is a natural expression. Compare: 起きる (okiru) = to get up (out of bed). In Japanese, waking and getting up are two distinct actions.

sentences.base_language: I am happy — or at least, I feel calm.
sentences.target_language: 私は幸せです——少なくとも、穏やかな気持ちです。
sentences.romanized: Watashi wa shiawase desu — sukunakutomo, odayaka na kimochi desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 気持ち (kimochi) = feeling, inner state. Expressing emotion through 気持ち or 気分 (kibun) is very natural in Japanese: 穏やかな気持ち (a calm feeling), いい気分 (feeling good). It's often more natural than stating the adjective directly.

sentences.base_language: Today is a good day. I need to go to the market near my neighbourhood.
sentences.target_language: 今日はいい日です。近所の市場に行く必要があります。
sentences.romanized: Kyō wa ii hi desu. Kinjo no ichiba ni iku hitsuyō ga arimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜する必要があります (〜suru hitsuyō ga arimasu) = need to do... The structure is: [verb dictionary form] + 必要があります. Casual alternatives: 〜しなければならない (must), 〜しないといけない (have to).

sentences.base_language: I put on my coat and walk straight ahead, then turn left at the corner.
sentences.target_language: コートを着て、まっすぐ歩き、角で左に曲がります。
sentences.romanized: Kōto wo kite, massugu aruki, kado de hidari ni magarimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 着る (kiru) = to put on / wear (upper body: shirts, coats, dresses). Japanese uses different verbs by body part: 履く (haku) for shoes and pants, かぶる (kaburu) for hats, する (suru) for accessories like ties and glasses.

sentences.base_language: The street is busy but not too noisy. The sun is hot on my face and shoulder.
sentences.target_language: 通りは賑やかですが、うるさすぎません。太陽が顔と肩に熱く当たっています。
sentences.romanized: Tōri wa nigiyaka desu ga, urusasugimase n. Taiyō ga kao to kata ni atsuku atatte imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜すぎます (sugimasu) = too much / excessively. Added to an adjective or verb stem: うるさすぎる (too noisy), 食べすぎる (eat too much), 高すぎる (too expensive). Very common in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: At the market, an old man named Paulo is a chef. He is also a musician — on weekends he plays his guitar in the park.
sentences.target_language: 市場では、パウロという老人が料理人をしています。彼は音楽家でもあり、週末には公園でギターを弾きます。
sentences.romanized: Ichiba de wa, Pauro to iu rōjin ga ryōrinin wo shite imasu. Kare wa ongakuka de mo ari, shūmatsu ni wa kōen de gitā wo hikimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜という (〜to iu) = called / named. パウロという老人 = an old man called Paulo. This pattern works for names and quoted concepts: どういう意味ですか (what does that mean), こういう場合 (a case like this).

sentences.base_language: Today he is cooking soup with onion, tomato, and fresh herbs. It smells delicious.
sentences.target_language: 今日は玉ねぎ、トマト、新鮮なハーブでスープを作っています。とてもいい香りがします。
sentences.romanized: Kyō wa tamanegi, tomato, shinsen na hābu de sūpu wo tsukutte imasu. Totemo ii kaori ga shimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜がします (ga shimasu) expresses sensory perception: いい香りがします (smells good), 音がします (there's a sound), 味がします (has a taste). This pattern describes something being perceived from outside — you can't control it.

sentences.base_language: "Good morning!" he says. "Would you like to taste this?" "Yes, please." The soup is hot, a little spicy, and very tasty.
sentences.target_language: 「おはようございます！」と彼は言います。「これを食べてみませんか？」「はい、ぜひ。」スープは熱くて、少しスパイシーで、とても美味しいです。
sentences.romanized: "Ohayō gozaimasu!" to kare wa iimasu. "Kore wo tabete mimasen ka?" "Hai, zehi." Sūpu wa atsukute, sukoshi supaisī de, totemo oishii desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜てみませんか (〜te mimasen ka) = would you like to try...? The 〜てみる pattern means 'try doing something and see': 食べてみる (try eating), 着てみる (try wearing). 〜ませんか is the invitation form: 一緒に行きませんか (shall we go together?).

sentences.base_language: I buy a big bowl and also some fruit — an apple, a banana, and an orange. I put them in my bag.
sentences.target_language: 大きなどんぶりを一杯買い、果物も買います——リンゴ、バナナ、オレンジ。それらをバッグに入れます。
sentences.romanized: Ōkina donburi wo ippai kai, kudamono mo kaimasu — ringo, banana, orenji. Sorera wo baggu ni iremasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 一杯 (ippai) = one bowl/cup (counter for liquid containers). Japanese counters change by object shape: 一個 (ikko) for round objects, 一本 (ippon) for long thin objects, 一枚 (ichimai) for flat objects, 一杯 (ippai) for bowls and cups.

sentences.base_language: Near the market there is a small café. I enter and sit at a table by the window.
sentences.target_language: 市場の近くに小さなカフェがあります。中に入り、窓際のテーブルに座ります。
sentences.romanized: Ichiba no chikaku ni chīsana kafe ga arimasu. Naka ni hairi, madogiwa no tēburu ni suwarimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: Location + に + があります = there is [something] at [place]. This places location before the subject. Compare: カフェは市場の近くにあります (The café is near the market) — topic first. Both are correct but emphasise different things.

sentences.base_language: A young waiter comes over. His name is Marcos. He is a student at the university and works here part-time.
sentences.target_language: 若いウェイターが来ます。彼の名前はマルコスです。大学の学生で、ここでアルバイトをしています。
sentences.romanized: Wakai weitā ga kimasu. Kare no namae wa Marukosu desu. Daigaku no gakusei de, koko de arubaito wo shite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: アルバイト (arubaito) = part-time job (from German 'Arbeit'). It's used so naturally in Japanese that フルタイム (full-time) and パートタイム (part-time) exist alongside it. Shortened form: バイト (baito).

sentences.base_language: "What do you recommend?" I ask. He thinks for a moment and smiles. "The cheese sandwich is very good today. And the orange juice."
sentences.target_language: 「何がおすすめですか？」と聞きます。彼は少し考えて、微笑みます。「今日はチーズサンドイッチがとてもいいですよ。オレンジジュースも。」
sentences.romanized: "Nani ga osusume desu ka?" to kikimasu. Kare wa sukoshi kangaete, hohoemiimasu. "Kyō wa chīzu sandoicchi ga totemo ii desu yo. Orenji jūsu mo."
sentences.grammarNote: おすすめ (osusume) = recommendation (honorific お + すすめ from すすめる, to recommend). It works as a noun and adjective: おすすめは何ですか (what do you recommend?), おすすめ料理 (recommended dish). よ at the end adds mild emphasis or assertion.

sentences.base_language: While I wait, I look out the window. A police officer is crossing the street. Two children are running in the park.
sentences.target_language: 待っている間、窓の外を眺めます。警察官が通りを渡っています。公園で子どもたちが走っています。
sentences.romanized: Matte iru aida, mado no soto wo nagamemasu. Keisatsukan ga tōri wo watatte imasu. Kōen de kodomotachi ga hashitte imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ている間 (〜te iru aida) = while doing... The 間 (aida) marks a time span: 待っている間 (while waiting), 食べている間 (while eating). Compare: 〜たとき (〜ta toki) = when [something happened], which marks a point in time rather than a span.

sentences.base_language: I feel something warm — not just the coffee in my hands, but something deeper. Joy, maybe. Or comfort.
sentences.target_language: 温かいものを感じます——手の中のコーヒーだけでなく、もっと深いものです。喜びかもしれません。あるいは、安らぎ。
sentences.romanized: Atatakai mono wo kanjimasu — te no naka no kōhī dake de naku, motto fukai mono desu. Yorokobi kamoshiremasen. Arui wa, yasuragi.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜だけでなく (dake de naku) = not only... Paired with も for 'not only... but also': コーヒーだけでなく、食事も (not just coffee, but the meal too). 〜かもしれません = might be / perhaps — expressing uncertainty about a feeling.

sentences.base_language: After my meal, I leave the café and walk to the library. I love the library — it is quiet and full of good things.
sentences.target_language: 食事の後、カフェを出て図書館へ歩きます。図書館が大好きです。静かで、良いものがたくさんあります。
sentences.romanized: Shokuji no ato, kafe wo dete toshokan e arukimasu. Toshokan ga daisuki desu. Shizuka de, yoi mono ga takusan arimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 大好き (daisuki) = love / really like (大 = big + 好き = like). Its opposite: 大嫌い (daikirai) = really hate. 〜が大好きです is how Japanese expresses strong affection for things or people. Plain 好き is milder ('I like it').

sentences.base_language: I am learning a new language — it is not always easy, and sometimes I feel stress, but I am calm today.
sentences.target_language: 新しい言語を学んでいます——いつも簡単ではなく、時々ストレスを感じますが、今日は穏やかです。
sentences.romanized: Atarashii gengo wo manande imasu — itsumo kantan de wa naku, tokidoki sutoresu wo kanjimasu ga, kyō wa odayaka desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ではなく (de wa naku) = not being... / it is not... Used to negate a noun or な-adjective and continue with a contrast: 簡単ではなく (not easy, and...). For い-adjectives: 難しくなく (not difficult, and...).

sentences.base_language: On my way home, I pass the hospital on the corner. My friend Amara is a nurse there.
sentences.target_language: 帰り道に、角の病院の前を通ります。友人のアマラはそこで看護師として働いています。
sentences.romanized: Kaeri michi ni, kado no byōin no mae wo tōrimasu. Yūjin no Amara wa soko de kangoshi to shite hataraite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜として (to shite) = as / in the role of. 看護師として働く = work as a nurse. This marks a capacity or role: 学生として (as a student), 友人として (as a friend), 代表として (as a representative). Very natural in professional contexts.

sentences.base_language: I arrive home just before dinner. I cook rice with chicken, a little salt, pepper, and oil.
sentences.target_language: 夕食の少し前に帰宅します。鶏肉と少量の塩、こしょう、油でご飯を作ります。
sentences.romanized: Yūshoku no sukoshi mae ni kitaku shimasu. Toriniku to shōryō no shio, koshō, abura de gohan wo tsukurimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 帰宅する (kitaku suru) = to return home (formal). More casual: 家に帰る (ie ni kaeru). 少量 (shōryō) = a small amount (formal/written). Casual equivalent: 少し (sukoshi). ご飯 (gohan) = cooked rice, but also used to mean 'a meal' in general.

sentences.base_language: I eat alone, but I do not feel lonely. I think about Paulo and his soup, Marcos and his smile, Amara and her courage.
sentences.target_language: 一人で食べますが、孤独は感じません。パウロとそのスープ、マルコスとその笑顔、アマラとその勇気のことを思います。
sentences.romanized: Hitori de tabemasu ga, kodoku wa kanjemasen. Pauro to sono sūpu, Marukosu to sono egao, Amara to sono yūki no koto wo omoimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜のことを思う = to think about [someone/something]. のこと adds specificity — it refers to the topic of thought. Compare: 彼を思う (I think of him, vaguely) vs 彼のことを思う (I think about him, as a subject of reflection).

sentences.base_language: I feel grateful. Here, in my small apartment, in this busy city, I am home.
sentences.target_language: 感謝の気持ちでいっぱいです。ここ、私の小さなアパートで、この賑やかな都市の中で、私は家にいます。
sentences.romanized: Kansha no kimochi de ippai desu. Koko, watashi no chīsana apāto de, kono nigiyaka na toshi no naka de, watashi wa ie ni imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜でいっぱいです (de ippai desu) = full of / filled with. 感謝の気持ちでいっぱい = filled with gratitude. This expressive pattern: 喜びでいっぱい (filled with joy), 希望でいっぱい (full of hope). The に in 家にいます marks location — います for living things.
